Question What association between gestational weight gain above below Institute Medicine guidelines maternal infant outcomes?
Findings this systematic review meta-analysis pregnancies, gestational weight gain below recommendations women) associated with higher risk small gestational (odds ratio [OR], 1.53) preterm birth (OR, 1.70) lower risk large gestational (OR, 0.59) macrosomia (OR, 0.60).
Gestational weight gain above recommendations (47%) associated with lower risk small gestational (OR, 0.66) preterm birth (OR, 0.77) higher risk large gestational (OR, 1.85), macrosomia (OR, 1.95), cesarean delivery (OR, 1.30).
Meaning Gestational weight gain below above Institute Medicine guidelines associated with higher risk some adverse maternal infant outcomes.
Importance Body mass index (BMI) gestational weight gain increasing globally.
Objective perform systematic review, meta-analysis, metaregression evaluate associations between gestational weight gain above below guidelines (gain 12.5-18 underweight women [BMI <18.5]; 11.5-16 normal-weight women [BMI 18.5-24.9]; 7-11 overweight women [BMI 25-29.9]; obese women [BMI ≥30]) maternal infant outcomes.
Data Sources Study Selection Search EMBASE, Evidence-Based Medicine Reviews, MEDLINE, MEDLINE In-Process between January 1999, February 2017, observational studies stratified prepregnancy category total gestational weight gain.
Data Extraction Synthesis Data were extracted independent reviewers.
Odds ratios (ORs) absolute risk differences (ARDs) live birth were calculated using random-effects model based subset studies with available data.
Main Outcomes Measures Primary outcomes were small gestational (SGA), preterm birth, large gestational (LGA).
Secondary outcomes were macrosomia, cesarean delivery, gestational diabetes mellitus.
Gestational weight gain below recommendations associated with higher risk (OR, 1.53 [95% 1.44-1.64]; ARD, [95% 4%-6%]) preterm birth (OR, 1.70 [1.32-2.20]; ARD, [3%-8%]) lower risk (OR, 0.59 [0.55-0.64]; ARD, [−10% −6%]) macrosomia (OR, 0.60 [0.52-0.68]; ARD, [−3% −1%]); cesarean delivery showed significant difference (OR, 0.98 [0.96-1.02]; ARD, [−2% 1%]).
Gestational weight gain above recommendations associated with lower risk (OR, 0.66 [0.63-0.69]; ARD, −3%; [−4% −2%]) preterm birth (OR, 0.77 [0.69-0.86]; ARD, [−2% −1%]) higher risk (OR, 1.85 [1.76-1.95]; ARD, [2%-5%]), macrosomia (OR, 1.95 [1.79-2.11]; ARD, [4%-9%]), cesarean delivery (OR, 1.30 [1.25-1.35]; ARD, [3%-6%]).
Gestational diabetes mellitus could evaluated because nature available data.
Conclusions Relevance this systematic review meta-analysis more than million pregnant women, gestational weight gain greater than recommendations gestational weight gain less than recommendations.
Gestational weight gain greater than less than guideline recommendations, compared with weight gain within recommended levels, associated with higher risk adverse maternal infant outcomes.
purpose this review meta-analysis compare gestational weight gain with guidelines from diverse international cohorts evaluate associations between gestational weight gain above below guidelines with maternal infant outcomes.
This systematic review, meta-analysis, metaregression prospectively registered with PROSPERO International Prospective Register Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO identifier CRD42015023325).
systematic search string relevant terms developed (eAppendix Supplement).

Observational studies published English assessing singleton pregnancies women aged years older were included.
Studies were included they presented data examining women prepregnancy category, stratified total gestational weight gain.
Studies that categorized mean weight gain week were excluded.
Only studies presenting odds ratios (ORs) stratified maternal gestational weight gain were included.
Studies that simultaneously adjusted categories gestational weight gain estimate independent associations weight change with outcomes were excluded because this review assess association gestational weight gain (specific each category) outcomes.
Studies meeting these criteria used different categories (eg, Metropolitan Life Insurance Tables, classifications, Chinese classifications11 [BMI underweight, <18.5; normal weight, 18.5-23.9; overweight, 24-28; obese, ≥28]) gestational weight gain categories (eg, 1990 IOM, 2009 IOM, population-specific, study-specific categories) classify participants.

Discrepancies were resolved consensus discussion with third reviewer (M.M.).
Methodological quality included studies assessed using Monash Centre Health Research Implementation evidence synthesis appraisal assessment tool.15,16 Individual quality items were assessed using descriptive approach including exposure outcome measures, reporting bias, confounding, conflict interest.
Each study classified low, medium, high risk bias.
Findings were synthesized target population characteristics, study type, outcome.
Outcome measures were produced each study calculating confidence intervals, using recommended gestational weight gain within each category reference.
When more studies assessed same outcome, results were pooled using both fixed- random-effects meta-analysis.
There were significant differences between fixed- random-effects analyses.
Random effects presented given heterogeneity among studies.
Extracted pooled individual outcomes were combined construct summary pooled ORs.
Crude data were used where possible, given variable control confounding factors.
However, some articles presented adjusted only.17-24 Absolute risk differences (ARDs) live birth were calculated from event rates (available subset studies) using random-effects meta-analysis.
Heterogeneity assessed using statistic, where indicated substantial heterogeneity.25 Metaregression performed investigate sources heterogeneity (percentage smokers during pregnancy, mean age, percentage nulliparous women).
Race/ethnicity data were available metaregression.
Where more studies were available, publication bias assessed using Egger test plots.26,27 Statistical significance defined 2-sided .05.
Statistical analysis used Stata software version (StataCorp LP).
subgroup analysis performed specific population groups identified priori (studies using Chinese Korean categories, presented herein).
Obesity subclasses were included after reviewing studies that stratified obesity class.
Tests trend based Cochran-Armitage test Stata were used assess trends this subgroup analysis.
